title: Gender Roles in the Family
examTip: The difference between 'instrumental' and 'expressive' roles (Parsons) is a classic AQA one-mark definition question — make sure you know both.
introTips: Know the key sociologists: Parsons (instrumental/expressive), Oakley (housework), Young and Willmott (symmetrical), Duncombe and Marsden (triple shift).
Be ready to evaluate — can you challenge Parsons with Oakley? Can you challenge Young and Willmott with real data?
Domestic violence statistics add weight to arguments about power inequality — 1 in 4 women affected.
intro: Who does the washing up? Who earns the money? These questions sound trivial but they reveal something fundamental about how power operates inside families — and how wider society is organised. Sociologists have argued for decades about whether gender roles in families are natural and functional, or socially constructed and deeply unequal. The answers matter enormously for women's lives, men's wellbeing, and children's development.

keyPoints.heading: Traditional gender roles: the functionalist view
keyPoints.content: Talcott Parsons (1955) argued the nuclear family has two complementary, biologically rooted roles. The instrumental role (male): breadwinner and economic provider; link between the family and wider society; rational and goal-oriented. The expressive role (female): emotional carer, homemaker, primary socialiser of children; warm and nurturing. Parsons claimed these roles are functionally necessary — they keep the family stable and allow it to perform its social functions. George Peter Murdock (1949) found a sexual division of labour in all 224 societies he studied and concluded it was natural and universal. Criticisms: These theories describe 1950s American middle-class families and treat one historical moment as universal. They completely ignore: women's ability to work; dual-income families (now the majority); same-sex families; the power imbalance these roles create; and the fact that millions of women find full-time domestic work unfulfilling and isolating (as Oakley showed).
writeBullets: Parsons (1955): instrumental role (male) — breadwinner, economic provider, rational
Parsons (1955): expressive role (female) — emotional carer, homemaker, primary socialiser
Murdock (1949): sexual division of labour found in all 224 societies — called it natural
Functionalists: these roles are complementary and functionally necessary for family stability
Criticism: based on 1950s USA; ignores dual-income families, same-sex families, women's capabilities
Criticism: the roles are socially constructed, not biological — they change across time and culture
keyPoints.memTip: Instrumental = In the world (breadwinner). Expressive = Emotion (carer). Two Es and two Is.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often mix up which role is which — a quick memory hook helps enormously here.

keyPoints.heading: Feminist critique: domestic labour and the dual burden
keyPoints.content: Ann Oakley (1974) carried out the first major sociological study of housework. She surveyed 40 London housewives and found: housework was monotonous, fragmented, and socially isolating; even women in paid employment did virtually all the housework at home. She challenged the view that housework is naturally women's work. The dual burden: women who work in paid employment also carry most of the domestic labour at home — effectively doing two jobs. Duncombe and Marsden (1995) extended this further with the concept of the triple shift: women do (1) paid employment, (2) housework and childcare, and (3) emotional labour — managing the emotional wellbeing of partners and children, absorbing stress and mediating conflict. Men consistently overestimate their contribution to domestic tasks (Oakley's methodological critique of Young and Willmott). Modern data confirms inequality persists: UK women do approximately 60% more unpaid work than men (ONS, 2022).
writeBullets: Oakley (1974): first study of housework — monotonous, isolating; women did it even when employed
Dual burden: women do paid work AND most domestic labour — effectively two full jobs
Triple shift (Duncombe and Marsden, 1995): paid work + housework/childcare + emotional labour
Emotional labour: managing wellbeing of partners and children — invisible but exhausting
Men consistently overestimate their domestic contribution (Oakley)
ONS (2022): UK women do ~60% more unpaid work than men
keyPoints.memTip: Oakley = the 'original' feminist study of housework. Triple shift = 3 jobs: Paid work, housework, emotions (PHE).
keyPoints.tutor_note: The triple shift concept is highly valued in AQA extended writing — it goes beyond 'women do more housework' to include emotional labour.

keyPoints.heading: The symmetrical family: evidence for change
keyPoints.content: Young and Willmott (1973) argued that the family was becoming more symmetrical: gender roles were converging, men were taking on more domestic tasks, and women were increasingly entering paid work. They identified three historical stages of family development: Stage 1 (pre-industrial — family as a unit of production; women central to economic life); Stage 2 (early industrial — sharp gender division; men in factories, women at home); Stage 3 (symmetrical family — tasks shared more equally; both partners working). They predicted Stage 3 was becoming the norm especially in working-class families. Criticisms by Oakley: Young and Willmott asked leading questions — 'Did you help your wife at the weekend?' — to which of course men said yes; this biased the results. Men exaggerate their domestic contribution. The change toward equality is real but far smaller and slower than Young and Willmott claimed. Modern data (ONS 2022) confirms significant inequality persists.
writeBullets: Young and Willmott (1973): families becoming more symmetrical — roles converging
Stage 1 (pre-industrial): family as production unit; Stage 2 (industrial): sharp gender division
Stage 3 (symmetrical): tasks more equally shared; both partners working
Claimed Stage 3 becoming norm in working-class families in 1970s
Oakley critique: leading questions biased results; men exaggerate contribution
ONS (2022): women still do ~60% more unpaid work — symmetry is exaggerated
keyPoints.memTip: Symmetrical = same on both sides. Young and Willmott thought we were getting there. Oakley said: not quite.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This is a perfect 'discuss' topic — use Young and Willmott as the optimistic view, then challenge with Oakley and ONS data.

keyPoints.heading: Power, decision-making and domestic violence
keyPoints.content: Edgell (1980) studied middle-class couples and found clear patterns in decision-making: major decisions (moving house, changing job, large financial purchases) were made by husbands or jointly with husbands having the final say. Minor everyday decisions (food shopping, children's clothing) were delegated to women. This reflects wider gender power imbalances — men control the strategically important decisions. Jan Pahl (1989) studied money management and found patterns vary by class and income: in lower-income households women more often managed day-to-day budgeting (but out of necessity, not power). Domestic violence: approximately 1 in 4 women and 1 in 6 men experience domestic abuse during their lifetime. Dobash and Dobash (1979), radical feminist sociologists, argued the family is the primary site of women's oppression: the vast majority of domestic violence is by men against women, reflecting patriarchal power structures — women are seen as the property of husbands. This is the most extreme expression of gender power inequality in the family.
writeBullets: Edgell (1980): major decisions made by husbands; minor decisions delegated to women
Jan Pahl (1989): money management patterns vary by class; lower-income women manage budgets out of necessity
Domestic violence: 1 in 4 women, 1 in 6 men experience abuse in lifetime
Dobash and Dobash (1979): family is primary site of women's oppression; most violence is men against women
Domestic violence reflects patriarchal power — women historically treated as property
Radical feminist view: the family cannot be reformed; patriarchy is the root cause
keyPoints.memTip: DEED = Decision-making (Edgell), Earnings management (Pahl), Emotional labour, Domestic violence (Dobash) — four dimensions of family power.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Domestic violence data is powerful supporting evidence for feminist arguments — don't shy away from including it.

keyPoints.heading: Changing gender roles: progress and limits
keyPoints.content: Women's employment has transformed over 50 years — the female employment rate was 72% in 2022 (male: 79%), and the gap is narrowing. Shared parental leave was introduced in 2015 (parents can share up to 50 weeks), but take-up by fathers remains extremely low (1–3% of eligible fathers). Same-sex couples (Weeks et al, 1999) often create more equal domestic arrangements — without pre-existing gender scripts, they negotiate roles more actively. The gender pay gap persists: the UK gap for full-time workers was 14.9% in 2022 (ONS). Despite progress, structural inequalities remain: the glass ceiling (invisible barrier to top positions — women hold only 27% of FTSE 100 board seats), occupational segregation (men dominate high-paid sectors like finance and tech; women dominate lower-paid caring professions), and pregnancy discrimination (illegal but still widespread). The picture is therefore one of genuine progress but persistent structural inequality — what feminists call 'unfinished revolution'.
writeBullets: Female employment rate: 72% (2022) — narrowing gap with men (79%)
Shared parental leave (2015): fathers can take up to 50 weeks; but only 1–3% do
Same-sex couples (Weeks et al, 1999): more equal domestic arrangements without gender scripts
Gender pay gap: 14.9% for full-time workers (ONS, 2022)
Glass ceiling: women only 27% of FTSE 100 board seats
Occupational segregation, pregnancy discrimination — structural barriers remain
keyPoints.memTip: Progress IS real — but GAPS remain: pay gap, board representation gap, domestic labour gap. Always acknowledge both.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The strongest exam answers acknowledge both real progress and persistent structural inequality — avoid one-sided answers.

tables.title: Sociological perspectives on gender roles in the family
rows: Functionalism
Complementary instrumental (male) and expressive (female) roles are natural and functionally necessary
Parsons (1955), Murdock (1949)
Based on 1950s USA norm; ignores power imbalance; roles are socially constructed not biological
Liberal Feminism
Unequal domestic labour is the problem; reform through equal pay, flexible working, better childcare; progress is happening
Oakley (1974)
Doesn't challenge the root patriarchal system; progress is too slow
Radical Feminism
Family is the primary site of patriarchal oppression; domestic violence is the extreme expression of male power
Dobash and Dobash (1979)
Overstates male dominance; ignores progress; many men are not violent
Postmodernism
Individuals now negotiate their own domestic arrangements; same-sex couples show gender roles are not fixed
Weeks et al (1999)
Overstates freedom of choice; ignores structural constraints on most people's lives
March of Progress
Family becoming more symmetrical; roles converging; genuine progress toward equality
Young and Willmott (1973)
Oakley: methodology was flawed; men exaggerate contribution; ONS data shows inequality persists

0.question: According to Parsons (1955), what is the 'expressive role' in the family?
options: Emotional carer, homemaker and primary socialiser of children — the nurturing role
Breadwinner and economic provider — the rational, goal-oriented role
The person who makes major household financial decisions
The link between the family and wider society through paid employment
0.correctFeedback: Correct! The expressive role (Parsons, 1955) is the emotional, nurturing, homemaking role — traditionally assigned to women.
0.wrongFeedback: That describes the instrumental role. The expressive role (Parsons, 1955) is the emotional, nurturing carer role — traditionally female in Parsons' model.

1.question: What is the 'triple shift' as identified by Duncombe and Marsden (1995)?
options: Women do paid employment, housework/childcare, AND emotional labour
Women work three different jobs to make ends meet
The three stages of family development identified by Young and Willmott
The three types of domestic violence identified by Dobash and Dobash
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Duncombe and Marsden (1995) argued women carry three burdens: paid work, domestic labour, and emotional labour — managing the emotional wellbeing of partners and children.
1.wrongFeedback: The triple shift (Duncombe and Marsden, 1995) refers to the three unpaid/paid roles women perform: paid employment, housework and childcare, and emotional labour.

3.question: Dobash and Dobash (1979) argued that domestic violence reflects which broader social structure?
options: Patriarchy — the system of male dominance in which women are treated as the property of husbands
Capitalism — violence is a result of economic stress and poverty
Secularisation — decline of religion has removed moral constraints on behaviour
Industrialisation — factory work created stress that men brought home
3.correctFeedback: Correct. Dobash and Dobash (1979) argued domestic violence is not random or individual — it reflects patriarchal structures where women are historically treated as male property.
3.wrongFeedback: Dobash and Dobash (1979) took a radical feminist view: domestic violence reflects patriarchy — the system of male dominance in which women are treated as the property of their husbands.

commonMistakes: Mixing up 'instrumental' and 'expressive' roles — always remember: instrumental = in the world (breadwinner), expressive = emotions (carer).
Saying Young and Willmott (1973) concluded families are fully equal — they said roles are becoming more symmetrical, not that they are equal.
Forgetting to criticise Oakley's methodology too — her sample of 40 London women is small and not representative of all UK women.
Presenting domestic violence as a marginal issue — the 1 in 4 women statistic shows it is widespread, not rare.
Assuming gender roles are only a feminist concern — functionalists, postmodernists and New Right thinkers all have views on this.
examTips: For questions about gender roles, always mention at least one named sociologist with a date and a specific finding — not just a general claim.
When using Oakley (1974) as evidence, specify what she found: housework was monotonous, fragmented, and isolating, and women did it even when in paid work.
The triple shift (Duncombe and Marsden, 1995) is a high-value concept — use it to show you go beyond the basic dual burden idea.
In evaluation, acknowledge progress: 'While inequality persists (ONS, 2022), women's employment rate has reached 72%, showing genuine change…'
revisionCardBullets: Parsons (1955): instrumental role (male) = breadwinner; expressive role (female) = emotional carer
Murdock (1949): sexual division of labour found in all 224 societies — called it natural
Oakley (1974): first housework study — monotonous, isolating; women did it even in paid work
Dual burden: women do paid work AND most domestic labour
Triple shift (Duncombe and Marsden, 1995): paid work + housework + emotional labour
Young and Willmott (1973): symmetrical family — roles converging; Stage 3 becoming norm
Oakley's critique: leading questions; men overestimate domestic contribution
Edgell (1980): major decisions by husbands; minor decisions delegated to women
Domestic violence: 1 in 4 women, 1 in 6 men (lifetime); Dobash and Dobash (1979): reflects patriarchy
Gender pay gap: 14.9% for full-time workers (ONS, 2022)
Glass ceiling: women hold only 27% of FTSE 100 board seats
Same-sex couples (Weeks et al, 1999): often more equal — no pre-existing gender scripts
